title: Sources of Energy
subject: science
audioScript: Good morning, everyone. Today we're opening Sources of Energy. Forget the heavy textbook tone for a moment — think of this chapter as a story about how things work in the real world. I'll use everyday examples first, then we'll connect them to what CBSE expects in the exam.

segments.teleprompter: Good morning, everyone. Today we're opening Sources of Energy. Forget the heavy textbook tone for a moment — think of this chapter as a story about how things work in the real world. I'll use everyday examples first, then we'll connect them to what CBSE expects in the exam.

Let's lock in the must-know words for Sources of Energy. Don't memorise long paragraphs — learn what each term means in one simple sentence. If you can explain these words to a friend, you're already halfway there.
In this part — core concepts — we'll keep it conversational. Chapter: Sources of Energy. I'll connect the idea to something you've seen in daily life before we go exam-deep.
Formulas can feel scary, but they're just shortcuts. For Sources of Energy, I'll say what each symbol means in plain language, then show when to use it — not just how to copy it from the board.
In this part — solved examples — we'll keep it conversational. Chapter: Sources of Energy. I'll connect the idea to something you've seen in daily life before we go exam-deep.
In this part — common mistakes — we'll keep it conversational. Chapter: Sources of Energy. I'll connect the idea to something you've seen in daily life before we go exam-deep.

Look at the diagram on screen. Trace it with your finger while I talk. For Sources of Energy, the picture is worth more than a page of notes — notice labels, direction arrows, and what changes from left to right.

Time to practise — not to panic. I've lined up about 3 questions on Sources of Energy, covering the main syllabus topics. Cover your notes, attempt each question in exam order, then check against the sections above.
Let's connect Sources of Energy to real life — not just the textbook. I'll walk through everyday examples for each syllabus topic: kitchen, lab, transport, or things you see in your neighbourhood.

Before you press play on any video about Sources of Energy, grab a notebook. Pause every two minutes and write one sentence in your own words. The links here are curated for Class 10 CBSE — NCERT-style explainers and one-shot revision.

This is your recap if you watched a video on Sources of Energy — or if you prefer reading first. I'll walk through the lesson flow: main topics, one common mistake, and what you should be able to explain without notes afterward.
Imagine I'm sitting beside you before the board exam. Here's Sources of Energy the way I'd explain it to a student who said 'I read it but didn't get it.' Slow pace, simple words, one idea at a time.
Quick recap of Sources of Energy. Three things to remember tonight: the big idea, one formula or rule, and one mistake to avoid. That's enough for a confident revision.

sections.title: 1. Concept Overview
sections.body:
- 
            Pralhad Venkatesh Joshi(Hon'ble Cabinet Minister) 
- 
            Shripad Yesso Naik(Hon'ble Minister of State) 
WHAT'S NEW
- Uploading of Bid Document on MNRE Website for wider publicity
- Clarification/ Amendment to ALMM OM dated 25.05.2026 – for RE Power Projects other than Rooftop Solar Projects
- Clarification on Solar Thermal Systems, Devices and Components (Quality Control) Order, 2024 dated 08th October 2024
- Guidelines for Quantifying Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Emissions from Offsite Water Drawl and Treatment, Ensuring Compliance with the Green Hydrogen Certification Scheme of India.
ACHIEVEMENTS
As per IRENA, Globally 3th position in overall Renewable Energy Capacity
53.7% Cumulative Installed Capacity from non-fossil fuel sources
Renewable Power generation increased nearly 2.5 times from 190 BU to 477 BU since 2014
Solar Power installed capacity increased approx 55.7 times from 2.82 GW to 157.05 GW since 2014
Wind capacity increased 2.7 times from 21 GW to 56.81 GW since 2014
As per IRENA, Globally 4th Position in Wind Power Capacity & 3rd in Solar Power Capacity
Record RE capacity addition of 132.5 GW in last 5 years
Innovative policy interventions such as IST…

teacherNarration.teleprompter: Good morning, everyone. Today we're opening Sources of Energy. Forget the heavy textbook tone for a moment — think of this chapter as a story about how things work in the real world. I'll use everyday examples first, then we'll connect them to what CBSE expects in the exam.

sections.title: 2. Key Definitions
sections.body:
**Key definitions:**

- National Institute of Solar Energy, an autonomous institution of Ministry of New and Renewable (MNRE), is the apex institute for research and development in the field Solar Energy.
- National Institute of Wind Energy (NIWE) established in Chennai in 1998 by the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE), Government of India is an autonomous research and development institution.
- Sardar Swaran Singh National Institute of Renewable Energy (SSS-NIBE), Kapurthala (Punjab) is an autonomous Institution of the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy.
- Indian Renewable Energy Development Agency Limited (IREDA) is a 'Navratna' non-banking financial institution under the administrative control of Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE). IREDA is engaged in promoting, developing and extending financial assistance for setting up projects.
- Ministry of New & Renewable Energy (MNRE) is the nodal agency at the central level for promotion of grid-connected and off-grid renewable energy in the country. Ministry’s programmes are implemented in close coordination with State Nodal Agencies (SNAs) for renewable energy (RE).
- A treaty-based international intergovernmental organization, International Solar Alliance (ISA), aims at mobilizing more than USD 1000 billion of investment needed by 2030 for the massive deployment of solar energy. Established by Prime Minister Narendra Modi and President of France Francois Hollande on November 30, 2015, ISA’s objective is to scale up solar energy, reduce the cost of solar power generation through aggregation of demand for solar finance, technologies, innovation, research and development, and capacity building.
-  The Sun has been worshiped as a life-giver to our planet since ancient times. The industrial ages gave us the understanding of sunlight as an energy source. India is endowed with vast solar energy potential. About 5,000 trillion kWh per year energy is incident over India’s land area with most parts receiving 4-7 kWh per sqm per day. Solar photovoltaic power can effectively be harnessed providing huge scalability in India. Solar also provides the ability to generate power on a distributed basis and enables rapid capacity addition with short lead times. Off-grid decentralized and low-temperature applications will be advantageous from a rural application perspective and meeting other energy needs for power, heating and cooling in both rural and urban areas. From an energy security perspective, solar is the most secure of all sources, since it is abundantly available. Theoretically, a small fraction of the total incident solar energy (if

sections.title: 8. Practice Questions
sections.body:
_Class 10 CBSE practice for **Sources of Energy**._

**Practice questions:**

1. 53.7% Cumulative Installed Capacity from non-fossil fuel sources

**More practice**

- **SHORT (2 marks)** — Explain **Sources of Energy** in 2–3 sentences. Use one example from Sources of Energy.
- **MCQ (1 mark)** — MCQ: Which statement about **Sources of Energy** is most accurate (Class 10 CBSE)? Review your notes, then justify your choice.
- **LONG (5 marks)** — Long answer: Describe the main ideas of **Sources of Energy**. Cover at least three syllabus topics and one real-life application.

sections.title: 11. Video Transcript Summary
sections.body:
_Teacher recap — what a strong Class 10 video lesson on **Sources of Energy** should cover:_

**Lesson flow (as if narrated on screen):**

1. **Hook** — Start with a daily-life example tied to Sources of Energy, not a textbook definition.
2. **core ideas** — Show a labelled diagram or quick demo, then state the CBSE definition in one sentence.
3. **definitions** — Show a labelled diagram or quick demo, then state the CBSE definition in one sentence.
4. **exam tips** — Show a labelled diagram or quick demo, then state the CBSE definition in one sentence.
5. **Common mistake** — Warn about the error students make most often in exams.
6. **Recap** — Three bullet points to remember before moving to practice questions.

**After watching, you should be able to:**

- Explain core ideas in everyday language.
- Draw or describe one diagram related to Sources of Energy.
- Attempt at least two short-answer questions without notes.

**Videos linked for this chapter:**

- 🔍 [CBSE class 10 Sources of Energy NCERT explained Hindi English](https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=CBSE+class+10+Sources+of+Energy+NCERT+explained+Hindi+English)
- 🔍 [Sources of Energy class 10 one shot revision](https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=Sources+of+Energy+class+10+one+shot+revision)
- 🔍 [NCERT Science class 10 Sources of Energy animation](https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=NCERT+Science+class+10+Sources+of+Energy+animation)
